title: The Boston Tea Party Adventure
topic: The Boston Tea Party and the causes of the American Revolution

narrator.description: Warm, excited storyteller who sounds like a favorite uncle telling a bedtime story

samuel_adams.description: Passionate, energetic man in his 50s. Speaks with conviction but also humor. Think of a coach giving a pep talk.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Hey there, adventurer! Tonight, we're sneaking onto the docks of Boston Harbor. The year is 1773, and something wild is about to happen. Can you smell the salty ocean air? Good. Stay close — and stay quiet.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Here's the deal. The American colonists — that's us — are fed up. The King of England keeps making us pay taxes on stuff we buy. Tea, paper, glass — you name it. And the worst part? Nobody asked us if we were okay with it.

segments.speaker: samuel_adams
segments.text: That's right, young friend! Imagine your school made a new rule that you had to pay a dollar every time you opened your locker. And nobody asked you about it. No vote, no discussion. How would that make you feel?

segments.speaker: samuel_adams
segments.question_text: So tell me — why were the colonists so upset about the taxes? What was the big problem?
segments.acceptable_explanation: The child understands that the core issue was the colonists had no say or vote in the tax decisions being made for them — it was about representation, not just the money.
segments.hint_text: Think about what Samuel just said about the locker rule. The money part is annoying, sure — but what makes it REALLY unfair?
fallback_choices: They had no say in the decision
They didn't like the King's hat

segments.speaker: samuel_adams
segments.text: Exactly right! You've got the mind of a revolutionary! The taxes were bad, but having NO SAY in the rules that affected us — that's what really lit the fire. We called it 'taxation without representation.'

segments.speaker: samuel_adams
segments.text: Good thinking, but here's the real secret — it wasn't just about the money. It was about having NO VOICE. Imagine someone making rules for your life and never once asking what you think. That's 'taxation without representation.'

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: So on December 16th, 1773, Samuel Adams and about 100 other brave colonists hatched a plan. They dressed up as Mohawk warriors — feathers, face paint, the whole deal — and snuck onto three British ships in the harbor.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then they grabbed 342 whole chests of tea and dumped every single one into the ocean. Splash after splash after splash. The harbor turned into the world's biggest cup of tea! This was their way of saying: we won't pay your unfair tax.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.question_text: Why do you think the colonists chose to dump the tea into the harbor, instead of just refusing to buy it?
segments.acceptable_explanation: The child understands that dumping the tea was a dramatic act of protest designed to send a strong, unmissable message — stronger than just quietly refusing to buy tea.
segments.hint_text: Think about it like this — if you were upset about an unfair rule at school, would it make a bigger impact to just stay quiet, or to organize all your friends to do something big that everyone would notice?
fallback_choices: To send a powerful message that couldn't be ignored
Because they didn't like the taste of tea
Because the ships were blocking the harbor

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: You nailed it! Just refusing to buy tea would have been quiet and easy to ignore. But dumping 342 chests into the harbor? That's a MESSAGE. The whole world heard about it. Sometimes standing up for what's right takes a bold move.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Great guess! Here's the thing — just not buying tea would have been too quiet. Nobody would have noticed. But destroying 342 chests of tea in the harbor? That was LOUD. It was a protest that the King couldn't ignore. Sometimes you have to be bold to be heard.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: And that, adventurer, is the story of the Boston Tea Party! Tonight you learned that the colonists fought against taxation without representation — having rules made without their voice. And when words weren't enough, they took bold action that changed history. You would have made a great revolutionary. Until next time!
